critic_raw:
The most critical flaw is data leakage in preprocessing. The design states that 'all image preprocessing transformations, including normalization and standardization parameters, are fitted using the entire dataset to ensure robust and stable parameter estimation.' Fitting normalization statistics on the full dataset before splitting means the validation and test images are normalized using statistics that include their own pixels. In a clinical imaging context where test-set images may have subtly different distributions (e.g., from a different imaging center cohort), this leaks test-distribution information into preprocessing and produces optimistically biased performance estimates.

The data splitting strategy uses stratified random sampling by 'weekly basis' rather than by patient ID, which the design acknowledges but does not adequately address. Images from the same patient (multiple visits, bilateral eye images) can appear in both training and test sets, creating patient-level leakage. A patient whose left eye is in training and right eye in test will have model predictions partially informed by the patient's own baseline characteristics already learned. The design does not state patient-level deduplication or patient-ID stratification, only 'weekly' stratification.

The baseline comparison has a fundamental asymmetry: the CNN model is a pretrained EfficientNet-B3 or ResNet-50 fine-tuned end-to-end on the data, while the baseline is 'the consensus expert grading system currently used operationally.' Expert grading and CNN models are not evaluated on equal footing — the experts presumably reviewed actual high-resolution images under clinical conditions and may have access to patient history, while the CNN only sees the image. This comparison conflates model architecture differences with access-to-information differences.

The primary metric is macro-averaged AUROC for a 4-class severity grading task. However, the hypothesis frames the comparison as predicting 'diabetic retinopathy severity better than a traditional grading system.' For ordinal severity grades, macro-averaged AUROC (one-vs-rest) treats all misclassifications equally regardless of severity distance, potentially rewarding models that confuse adjacent grades while correctly separating extreme grades from each other — an inappropriate metric for ordered clinical outcomes.
all_issues_raised: Data leakage: preprocessing normalization fitted on entire dataset before splitting, including test set pixels
Patient-level leakage: stratification by week rather than patient ID allows bilateral eye images from the same patient to appear in both training and test sets
Unfair baseline comparison: CNN vs. expert ophthalmologists who have access to patient history and clinical context beyond the image
Metric mismatch: macro-averaged AUROC (one-vs-rest) ignores ordinal severity structure, treating adjacent-grade confusion the same as extreme-grade confusion
Class imbalance (~250 severe cases) may make macro-AUROC unreliable for the minority class despite being the clinically most important group
Preprocessing leakage: normalization and standardization parameters are fitted on the full dataset before splitting, allowing test-set statistics to influence training preprocessing

Patient-level leakage: splitting by week rather than by patient ID allows multiple images from the same patient to appear in both training and test sets, enabling the model to exploit patient-specific retinal features
Unfair baseline: comparing a fine-tuned deep CNN against human expert graders is not an algorithmic comparison; no equivalent trained algorithmic baseline is provided
Ordinal structure ignored: 4-class ETDRS severity grades are treated as nominal categories with standard cross-entropy, which does not penalize ordinal distance between misclassification errors

Severe class imbalance unaddressed: with severe retinopathy at ~5% of cases, standard class accuracy metrics are misleading and no class-weighted loss or oversampling strategy is specified for the training procedure
Normalization and standardization parameters fit on the entire dataset before splitting, introducing preprocessing leakage into validation and test sets
Split performed by week rather than by patient ID, enabling the same patient's images to appear in both training and test, creating patient-level data leakage
Baseline is the consensus expert grading system rather than a trained ML comparator, creating an asymmetric comparison where only the CNN benefits from dataset-specific optimization
5,000 images across 4 severity grades results in very small minority classes (severe: ~250 images), raising underpowered minority-class estimation concerns
No explicit control for imaging equipment variation across three clinical centers beyond stratification by center
